revision.comment: initial text of a stub article -- 1st steps in a constructive direction
revision.text:
{{nihongo|'''Niukawakami Shrine'''|丹生川上神社|Niukawakami Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at [[Nara, Nara|Nara]] in [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]], [[Japan]].
The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Niukawakami Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
== See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]]
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References== * Breen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-824-82363-X; 13-ISBN 978-0-824-82363-4 * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449]
{{DEFAULTSORT:Niukawakami Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]]
{{Shinto-stub}}
[[ja:丹生川上神社]]

[[File:Niukawakami Shrine.jpg|thumb|right|190px|Niukawakami Shrine at [[Nara, Nara|Nara]] in [[Japan]].]]
{{nihongo|'''Niukawakami Shrine'''|丹生川上神社|''Niukawakami Jinja''}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at [[Nara, Nara|Nara]] in [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]], [[Japan]].
The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Niukawakami Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
== See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]]
==Notes== {{reflist}}
==References== * Breen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-824-82363-X; 13-ISBN 978-0-824-82363-4 * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449]
{{DEFAULTSORT:Niukawakami Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]]
{{Shinto-stub}}
[[ja:丹生川上神社]]

[[File:Niukawakami Shrine.jpg|thumb|right|190px|Niukawakami Shrine at [[Nara, Nara|Nara]] in [[Japan]].]]
{{nihongo|'''Niukawakami Shrine'''|丹生川上神社|''Niukawakami Jinja''}}, also known as Nibukawakami Jinja, is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at [[Nara, Nara|Nara]] in [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]], [[Japan]].
The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Niukawakami Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Nibukawakami Jinja was officially designated one of the ''Kanpei-taisha'' (官幣大社), meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines. <ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124.</ref>
== See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]]
==Notes== {{reflist|2}}
==References== * Breen, John and Mark Teeuwen. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-824-82363-X; 13-ISBN 978-0-824-82363-4 * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887]

[[File:Niukawakami Shrine.jpg|thumb|right|190px|Niukawakami Shrine at [[Nara, Nara|Nara]] in [[Japan]].]]
{{nihongo|'''Niukawakami Shrine'''|丹生川上神社|''Niukawakami Jinja''}}, also known as Nibukawakami Jinja, is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at [[Nara, Nara|Nara]] in [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]], [[Japan]].
==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].<ref>Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.]</ref> In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Niukawakami Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Nibukawakami Jinja was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines. <ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124.</ref>
== See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]
==Notes== {{reflist|2}}
==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [http://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. 10-ISBN 0-8248-2363-X; 13-ISBN 978-0-8248-2363-4 * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887]

[[Higashiyoshino, Nara|Higashiyoshino]] [[Yoshino District]]
Nara prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|34|23|25|N|135|59|11|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=inline,title}} | location_map = Japan | map_caption = | website = http://www.niukawakami-jinja.jp/ }} {{nihongo|'''Niukawakami Shrine'''|丹生川上神社|''Niukawakami Jinja''}}, also known as Nibukawakami Jinja, is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at [[Higashiyoshino, Nara|Higashiyoshino]] in [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]], [[Japan]]. ==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Niukawakami Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. From 1871 through 1946, the Nibukawakami Jinja was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Niukawakami Shrine
丹生川上神社 | image = Niukawakami Shrine.jpg | caption = Niukawakami Shrine at [[Nara prefecture|Nara]] in [[Japan]]. | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|23|25|N|135|59|11|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Mizuhanome]] | founded_by = | established = 675 | date_destroyed = | location = 968 Ōaza Omura
[[Higashiyoshino, Nara|Higashiyoshino]] [[Yoshino District]]
Nara prefecture | website = {{URL|http://www.niukawakami-jinja.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | festival = 16 October | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Niukawakami Shrine'''|丹生川上神社|''Niukawakami Jinja''}}, also known as Nibukawakami Jinja, is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at [[Higashiyoshino, Nara|Higashiyoshino]] in [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]], [[Japan]]. ==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Niukawakami Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. From 1871 through 1946, the Nibukawakami Jinja was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpo-taisya]] == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Niukawakami Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] {{Shinto-stub}}

{{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Niukawakami Shrine
丹生川上神社 | image = Niukawakami Shrine.jpg | caption = Niukawakami Shrine at [[Nara prefecture|Nara]] in [[Japan]]. | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|23|25|N|135|59|11|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Mizuhanome]] | founded_by = | established = 675 | date_destroyed = | location = 968 Ōaza Omura
[[Higashiyoshino, Nara|Higashiyoshino]] [[Yoshino District]]
Nara prefecture | website = {{URL|http://www.niukawakami-jinja.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | festival = 16 October | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Niukawakami Shrine'''|丹生川上神社|''Niukawakami Jinja''}}, also known as Nibukawakami Jinja, is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at [[Higashiyoshino, Nara|Higashiyoshino]] in [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]], [[Japan]]. ==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Niukawakami Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. From 1871 through 1946, the Nibukawakami Jinja was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&dq=nijunisha&source=gbs_navlinks_s ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Niukawakami Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] {{Shinto-stub}}

(2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Niukawakami Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. From 1871 through 1946, the Nibukawakami Jinja was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). 丹生川上神社 | image = Niukawakami Shrine.jpg | caption = Niukawakami Shrine at [[Nara prefecture|Nara]] in [[Japan]]. | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|23|25|N|135|59|11|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Mizuhanome]] | founded_by = | established = 675 | date_destroyed = | location = 968 Ōaza Omura
[[Higashiyoshino, Nara|Higashiyoshino]] [[Yoshino District]]
Nara prefecture | website = {{URL|http://www.niukawakami-jinja.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | festival = 16 October | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Niukawakami Shrine'''|丹生川上神社|''Niukawakami Jinja''}}, also known as Nibukawakami Jinja, is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at [[Higashiyoshino, Nara|Higashiyoshino]] in [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]], [[Japan]]. ==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Niukawakami Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. From 1871 through 1946, the Nibukawakami Jinja was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. == Composition == The shrine has two sub-shrines * {{Interlanguage link|Niukawakami Upper Shrine|ja|丹生川上神社上社}} * {{Interlanguage link|Niukawakami Lower Shrine|ja|丹生川上神社下社}} == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). (1959). [[Higashiyoshino, Nara|Higashiyoshino]] [[Yoshino District]]
Nara prefecture | website = {{URL|http://www.niukawakami-jinja.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | festival = 16 October | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Niukawakami Shrine'''|丹生川上神社|''Niukawakami Jinja''}}, also known as Nibukawakami Jinja, is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at [[Higashiyoshino, Nara|Higashiyoshino]] in [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]], [[Japan]]. ==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Niukawakami Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. From 1871 through 1946, the Nibukawakami Jinja was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. == Composition == The shrine has two sub-shrines * {{Interlanguage link|Niukawakami Upper Shrine|ja|丹生川上神社上社}} * {{Interlanguage link|Niukawakami Lower Shrine|ja|丹生川上神社下社}} == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Niukawakami Shrine
丹生川上神社 | image = Niukawakami Shrine.jpg | caption = Niukawakami Shrine at [[Nara prefecture|Nara]] in [[Japan]]. | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|23|25|N|135|59|11|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Mizuhanome]] | founded_by = | established = 675 | date_destroyed = | location = 968 Ōaza Omura
[[Higashiyoshino, Nara|Higashiyoshino]] [[Yoshino District]]
Nara prefecture | website = {{URL|http://www.niukawakami-jinja.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | festival = 16 October | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Niukawakami Shrine'''|丹生川上神社|''Niukawakami Jinja''}}, also known as Nibukawakami Jinja, is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at [[Higashiyoshino, Nara|Higashiyoshino]] in [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]], [[Japan]]. ==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Niukawakami Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. From 1871 through 1946, the Nibukawakami Jinja was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. == Composition == The shrine has two sub-shrines * {{Interlanguage link|Niukawakami Upper Shrine|ja|丹生川上神社上社}} * {{Interlanguage link|Niukawakami Lower Shrine|ja|丹生川上神社下社}} == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Niukawakami Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. From 1871 through 1946, the Nibukawakami Jinja was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). Nara prefecture | website = {{URL|http://www.niukawakami-jinja.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | festival = 16 October | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Niukawakami Shrine'''|丹生川上神社|''Niukawakami Jinja''}}, also known as Nibukawakami Jinja, is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at [[Higashiyoshino, Nara|Higashiyoshino]] in [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]], [[Japan]]. ==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Niukawakami Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. From 1871 through 1946, the Nibukawakami Jinja was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. == Composition == The shrine has two sub-shrines * {{Interlanguage link|Niukawakami Upper Shrine|ja|丹生川上神社上社}} * {{Interlanguage link|Niukawakami Lower Shrine|ja|丹生川上神社下社}} == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Niukawakami Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. From 1871 through 1946, the Nibukawakami Jinja was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). Nara prefecture | website = {{URL|http://www.niukawakami-jinja.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | festival = 16 October | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Niukawakami Shrine'''|丹生川上神社|''Niukawakami Jinja''}}, also known as Nibukawakami Jinja, is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at [[Higashiyoshino, Nara|Higashiyoshino]] in [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]], [[Japan]]. ==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Niukawakami Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. From 1871 through 1946, the Nibukawakami Jinja was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. == Composition == The shrine has two sub-shrines * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Niukawakami Upper Shrine|ja|丹生川上神社上社}} * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Niukawakami Lower Shrine|ja|丹生川上神社下社}} == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Niukawakami Shrine
丹生川上神社 | image = Niukawakami Shrine.jpg | caption = Niukawakami Shrine at [[Nara prefecture|Nara]] in [[Japan]]. | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|23|25|N|135|59|11|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Mizuhanome]] | founded_by = | established = 675 | date_destroyed = | location = 968 Ōaza Omura
[[Higashiyoshino, Nara|Higashiyoshino]] [[Yoshino District]]
Nara prefecture | website = {{URL|http://www.niukawakami-jinja.jp/}} | architecture_style = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | festival = 16 October | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Niukawakami Shrine'''|丹生川上神社|''Niukawakami Jinja''}}, also known as Nibukawakami Jinja, is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at [[Higashiyoshino, Nara|Higashiyoshino]] in [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]], [[Japan]]. ==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Niukawakami Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. From 1871 through 1946, the Nibukawakami Jinja was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. == Composition == The shrine has two sub-shrines * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Niukawakami Upper Shrine|ja|丹生川上神社上社}} * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Niukawakami Lower Shrine|ja|丹生川上神社下社}} == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Niukawakami Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Niukawakami Shrine}} (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Niukawakami Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. From 1871 through 1946, the Nibukawakami Jinja was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). {{nihongo|'''Niukawakami Shrine'''|丹生川上神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Yamato Province]].

{{nihongo|'''Niukawakami Shrine'''|丹生川上神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Yamato Province]].

{{nihongo|'''Niukawakami Shrine'''|丹生川上神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Yamato Province]].

{{nihongo|'''Niukawakami Shrine'''|丹生川上神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Yamato Province]].

{{nihongo|'''Niukawakami Shrine'''|丹生川上神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Yamato Province]].

{{nihongo|'''Niukawakami Shrine'''|丹生川上神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Yamato Province]].

{{nihongo|'''Niukawakami Shrine'''|丹生川上神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Yamato Province]].

{{nihongo|'''Niukawakami Shrine'''|丹生川上神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Yamato Province]].

{{nihongo|'''Niukawakami Shrine'''|丹生川上神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Yamato Province]].

{{nihongo|'''Niukawakami Shrine'''|丹生川上神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Yamato Province]].

{{nihongo|'''Niukawakami Shrine'''|丹生川上神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Yamato Province]].

{{nihongo|'''Niukawakami Shrine'''|丹生川上神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Yamato Province]].
